elements.sent-2411-001: Iorio di Italia 68, I-70125, Bari (BA), Italy.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recently, a perturbative calculation to the first post--Newtonian order has shown that the analytically worked out Lense--Thirring precession of the orbital angular momentum of a test particle following a circular path around a massive spinning primary is able to explain the measured features of the jet precession of the supermassive black hole at the centre of the giant elliptical galaxy M87.
elements.sent-2411-003: It is shown that also the hole's mass quadrupole moment $Q_2$, as given by the no--hair theorems, has a dynamical effect which cannot be neglected, as, instead, done so far in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-004: New allowed regions for the hole's dimensionless spin parameter $a^\ast$ and the effective radius $r_0$ of the accretion disk, assumed tightly coupled with the jet, are obtained by including both the Lense--Thirring and the quadrupole effects in the dynamics of the effective test particle modeling the accretion disk.
elements.sent-2411-005: One obtains that, by numerically integrating the resulting averaged equations for the rates of change of the angles $\eta$ and $\phi$ characterizing the orientation of the orbital angular momentum with $a^\ast = +0.98$ and $r_0=14.1$ gravitational radii, it is possible to reproduce, both quantitatively and qualitatively, the time series for them recently measured with the Very Long Baseline Interferometry technique.
elements.sent-2411-006: Instead, the resulting time series produced with $a^\ast = -0.95$ and $r_0=16$ gravitational radii turn out to be out of phase with respect to the observationally determined ones, while maintaining the same amplitudes.
elements.sent-2411-007: It has recently been proven \cite{2024arXiv241108686I that a simple perturbative calculation to the first post--Newtonian (1pN) order of the Lense--Thirring (LT) effect on the orbit of a test particle moving about a massive spinning object is able to reproduce, both qualitatively and quantitatively, several features of the jet precession in the supermassive black hole (SMBH) M87$^\ast$ recently measured with the Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) technique \cite{2023Natur.621..711C .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Aim of this paper is to show that, actually, also the mass quadrupole moment\footnote{The possible impact of electric charge was recently investigated \cite{2024arXiv241107481M in the framework of the Kerr--Newman metric \cite{1965JMP.....6..915N,1965JMP.....6..918N as well.}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The imprints of the precession of the latter on the SMBH-accretion disk system was recently investigated in \cite{2024arXiv241010965C .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The introduction of the hole's quadrupole does not spoil the agreement between the perturbative analytical calculation and the characteristics measured in \cite{2023Natur.621..711C , yielding to different allowed regions for the hole's spin parameter and the radius of the effective circular orbit modeling the precessing disk.}
elements.sent-2411-011: As a result, the celebrated \virg{no--hair (NH) theorems \cite{1967PhRv..164.1776I,1971PhRvL..26..331C,1975PhRvL..34..905R receive a strong support.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: According to the latter ones, the mass and the spin moments $\mathcal{M ^\ell$ and $\mathcal{J ^\ell$ of degree $\ell$ of a rotating BH \cite{1970Natur.226...64B , whose external spacetime is described by the Kerr metric \cite{1963PhRvL..11..237K,2015CQGra..32l4006T , are connected by the relation \eqi \mathcal{M ^\ell + i\,\mathcal{J ^\ell= M \ton{i\,\rp{J {cM ^\ell,\lb{nohair \eqf where $i:=\sqrt{-1}$ is the imaginary unit, $c$ is the speed of light, and $M$ and $J$ are the hole's mass and spin angular momentum, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: From \rfr{nohair , it turns out that the odd mass moments and even spin moments are identically zero.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In particular, the hole's mass moment $\mathcal{M ^0$ of degree $\ell=0$ is its mass, while its spin dipole moment $\mathcal{J}$ of degree $\ell=1$ is its spin angular momentum.}
elements.sent-2411-015: For a Kerr BH, it is \cite{1986bhwd.book.....S \eqi J =\chi \rp{M^2 G {c ,\,\left|\chi\right|\leq 1,\lb{Jei \eqf where $G$ is the Newtonian constant of gravitation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Furthermore, the mass quadrupole moment $\mathcal{M ^2$ of degree $\ell=2$, renamed $Q_2$, is \eqi Q_2 = -\rp{J^2 {c^2 M .\lb{Q2 \eqf If $\left|\chi\right|>1$, a naked singularity \cite{1973CMaPh..34..135Y,1991PhRvL..66..994S without a horizon would occur, implying the possibility of causality violations because of closed timelike curves.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Although not yet proven, the cosmic censorship conjecture \cite{1999JApA...20..233P,2002GReGr..34.1141P states that naked singularities may not be formed via the gravitational collapse of a material body.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The dimensionless parameter $\chi$ can also be viewed as the second characteristic length\footnote{Sometimes, the symbol $a$ is used for $J/\ton{cM}$ itself, in which case it is dimensionally a length.}
elements.sent-2411-019: In BH studies, $\chi$ is often denoted with $a$, which is the same symbol usually adopted in celestial mechanics and astrodynamics to denote the semimajor axis of a generally elliptical orbit of a test particle.
elements.sent-2411-020: The organization of the paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-021: Section \ref{sect:model offers a review of the perturbative analytical model of the LT and mass quadrupole effects, expressed in terms of the both the Keplerian orbital elements and in vectorial form, for a generic orientation of the primary's spin axis in space.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In Section \ref{sect:application , the previous results are successfully applied to the measured precession of the jet of M87$^\ast$, assumed strongly coupled with that of the accretion disk.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Section \ref{fine summarizes the findings and offers conclusions.}
elements.section-2411-002: The analytical model for the orbital precessions
elements.sent-2411-024: Their LT rates of change averaged over one orbital revolution and valid for an arbitrary orientation of the primary's spin axis $\boldsymbol{\hat{k }$ turn out to be \begin{equation}\dot I^LT \lb{dotILT} = \rp{2GJ\ton{\boldsymbol{\hat{k}}\boldsymbol\cdot\boldsymbol{\hat{l}}}}{c^2 a^3\ton{1 - e^2}^{3/2}},\acap \dot{\Omega}^LT \lb{dotOLT} = \rp{2GJ\csc I\ton{\boldsymbol{\hat{k}}\boldsymbol\cdot\boldsymbol{\hat{m}}}}{c^2 a^3\ton{1 - e^2}^{3/2}},\end{equation} where, for a Kerr BH, $J$ is given by \rfr{Jei .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Furthermore, $a$ and $e$ are the orbit's semimajor axis and eccentricity, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-026: The unit vectors $\boldsymbol{\hat{l }$ and $\boldsymbol{\hat{m }$, which lie in the orbital plane and are mutually orthogonal, are defined as \cite{Sof89,1991ercm.book.....B,SoffelHan19,2024gpno.book.....I \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{\hat{l}} \lb{elle} :=\grf{\cos\Omega,\sin\Omega,0},\acap \boldsymbol{\hat{m}} \lb{emme} := \grf{-\cos I \sin\Omega, \cos I \cos\Omega, \sin I}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The mean rates of changes of the same orbital elements caused by the oblateness of a massive primary, valid for a generic orientation of $\boldsymbol{\hat{k }$, can be calculated with the same approach obtaining \cite{2024gpno.book.....I \begin{equation}\dot I^{Q_2} \lb{dotIQ2} = -\rp{3}{2}\nk J_2\ton{\rp{R}{p}}^2\ton{\boldsymbol{\hat{k}}\boldsymbol\cdot\boldsymbol{\hat{h}}}\ton{\boldsymbol{\hat{k}}\boldsymbol\cdot\boldsymbol{\hat{l}}},\acap \dot{\Omega}^{Q_2} \lb{dotOQ2} = -\rp{3}{2}\nk J_2\csc I\ton{\rp{R}{p}}^2\ton{\boldsymbol{\hat{k}}\boldsymbol\cdot\boldsymbol{\hat{h}}}\ton{\boldsymbol{\hat{k}}\boldsymbol\cdot\boldsymbol{\hat{m}}}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: In \rfrs{dotIQ2 {dotOQ2 , $\nk:=\sqrt{GM/a^3}$ is the Keplerian mean motion, $p:=a\ton{1-e^2}$ is the semilatus rectum, the unit vector $\boldsymbol{\hat{h }$, directed along the orbital angular momentum and orthogonal to both $\boldsymbol{\hat{l }$ and $\boldsymbol{\hat{m }$ in such a way that $\boldsymbol{\hat{l }\boldsymbol\times\boldsymbol{\hat{m = \boldsymbol{\hat{h }$ holds, is defined as \cite{Sof89,1991ercm.book.....B,SoffelHan19,2024gpno.book.....I \eqi \boldsymbol{\hat{h \lb{acca := \grf{\sin I \sin\mathit{\Omega , -\sin I \cos\mathit{\Omega , \cos I , \eqf $R$ is the equatorial radius of the primary, and $J_2$ is its dimensionless mass quadrupole moment.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The latter one must be expressed in terms of its dimensional counterpart $Q_2$ having dimensions of a mass times a length squared as \eqi J_2 := -\rp{Q_2 {M R^2 , \eqf which, for a Kerr BH, is given by \rfr{Q2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The resulting NH orbital rates are \begin{equation}\dot I^NH \lb{dotI} = \dot I^LT + \dot I^{Q_2},\acap \dot{\Omega}^NH \lb{dotO} = \dot{\Omega}^LT + \dot{\Omega}^{Q_2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-031: From \rfrs{precNH {precQ2 , it can be noted that the orbital angular momentum precesses around the primary's spin axis.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Such results are in agreement with \cite{1974PhRvD..10.1340B,1975PhRvD..12..329B , where the term accounting also for the simultaneous precession of the Laplace--Runge--Lenz vector \cite{Gold80,Taff85 , of no interest here, was included in the total precessional velocity.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: By considering the total NH precession velocity, given by \rfrs{precNH {precQ2 , as a function of $a^\ast$ and the effective radius $r_0$ of the precessing accretion disk, it is possible to plot its absolute value by imposing the condition that its graph is comprised within the upper an lower measured values of the absolute value of the measured precession velocity \cite{2023Natur.621..711C \eqi \left|\omega^\mathrm{exp _\mathrm{p}\right| = 0.56\pm 0.02\,\mathrm{rad\,yr ^{-1 , \eqf i.e., \eqi 0.54\,\mathrm{rad\,yr ^{-1}\leq \left|\Omega_\mathrm{d ^\mathrm{NH}\ton{a^\ast,r_0}\right|\leq 0.58\,\mathrm{rad\,yr ^{-1 .\lb{condiz \eqf The sets of the values of $a^\ast$ and $r_0$ satisfying the condition of \rfr{condiz form allowed regions in the $\grf{a^\ast,r_0}$ plane depicted in pale yellow in Figure \ref{figure:allowed:tot .}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{tabular}{c} \includegraphics[width = 15 cm]{Allowedplusminus.pdf} \end{tabular} \caption{Entire allowed regions in the $\grf{a^\ast,r_0}$ plane corresponding to the condition that the graphs of $\left|\Omega_\mathrm{d}^\mathrm{LT}\ton{a^\ast,r_0}\right|$ and $\left|\Omega_\mathrm{d}^\mathrm{NH}\ton{a^\ast,r_0}\right|$ remain confined between the upper and lower experimentally allowed values of $\left|\omega_\mathrm{p}^\mathrm{exp}\right|$, i.e., $0.54\,\mathrm{rad\,yr}^{-1}\leq \left|\Omega_\mathrm{d}^\mathrm{LT}\ton{a^\ast,r_0}\right|,\left|\Omega^\mathrm{NH}_\mathrm{d}\ton{a^\ast,r_0}\right|\leq 0.58\,\mathrm{rad\,yr}^{-1}$. The minimum value for $r_0$ has been taken from the largest expected radius of the TISCO orbit for $\psi_\mathrm{jet}\simeq 0^\circ$, as per Figure 1 of \cite{2024PhRvD.109b4029A}. }\label{figure:allowed:tot}
elements.sent-2411-034: In it, also the permitted regions obtained by considering solely the LT effect are shown in pale blue as well \cite{2024arXiv241108686I .}
elements.sent-2411-035: It turns out that both the LT and the $\mathrm{LT +Q_2$ regions overlap in the no--rotation limit $\left|a^\ast\right|\rightarrow 0$, as expected.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Instead, they separate when the hole's spin parameter tends to unity.
elements.sent-2411-037: While both the allowed branches are equal and symmetric with respect to the $a^\ast=0$ axis in the LT--only case, the inclusion of the SMBH's quadrupole breaks such a symmetry allowing for larger values of $r_0$ corresponding to the retrograde rotation of the hole ($a^\ast<0$) with respect to the prograde case ($a^\ast>0$).
elements.sent-2411-038: As a general feature, for prograde hole's rotation, $Q_2$ generally tends to shrink the disk (right branches), while the opposite occurs for retrograde hole's rotation (left branches) with respect to the LT--only scenario.
elements.sent-2411-039: The minimum physically admissible value for $r_0$ was taken from Figure 1 of \cite{2024PhRvD.109b4029A which depicts the radius of the tilted\footnote{The tilt is with respect to the hole's equator.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: It turns out that, for slightly tilted orbits as in the case of M87$^\ast$ ($\psi^\mathrm{M87^\ast _\mathrm{jet}\simeq 1.25^\circ$ \cite{2023Natur.621..711C ), the radius of the TISCO is \begin{equation}r_TISCO \simeq 8 R_g\,\ton{a^\ast\simeq -1},\acap r_TISCO \simeq 2 R_g\,\ton{a^\ast\simeq 1}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Figure \ref{figure:allowed_plus_minus shows two insets of Figure \ref{figure:allowed:tot corresponding to $\left|a^\ast\right|\lesssim 1$, in agreement with most of the constraints on it existing in the literature \cite{1998AJ....116.2237K,2008ApJ...676L.109W,2009ApJ...699..513L,2012Sci...338..355D,2017MNRAS.470..612F,2018MNRAS.479L..65S,2019ApJ...880L..26N,2019MNRAS.489.1197N,2020AnP...53200480G,2020MNRAS.492L..22T,2023Astro...2..141D .}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{tabular}{c} \includegraphics[width = 10 cm]{Allowedplus.pdf} \includegraphics[width = 10 cm]{Allowedminus.pdf} \end{tabular} \caption{Insets of Figure \ref{figure:allowed:tot} for positive (upper panel) and negative (lower panel) values of the SMBH's spin parameter close to unity. The pale blue and pale yellow allowed regions correspond to the LT--only and $\mathrm{LT}+Q_2$ cases, respectively. }\label{figure:allowed_plus_minus}
elements.sent-2411-042: In such regimes, the impact of $Q_2$ appears quite evident with respect to the case in which only the LT effect is taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-043: Figures \ref{figure:etaphi_plus to \ref{figure:etaphi_minus display the numerically integrated NH time series of the time--dependent angles $\eta\ton{t}$ and $\phi\ton{t}$ determining the orientation of the disk's orbital angular momentum in space; in the language of celestial mechanics and astrodynamics, they correspond to the longitude of the ascending node $\mathit{\Omega}$ and the inclination $I$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Their signatures were theoretically obtained by simultaneously integrating the averaged equations for the rates of change of \rfrs{dotI {dotO by adopting the values $a^\ast = +0.98, r_0=14.1\,R_g$ (Figure \ref{figure:etaphi_plus ) and $a^\ast = -0.95, r_0=16\,R_g$ (Figure \ref{figure:etaphi_minus ), contained in the pale yellow regions of the upper and lower panels of Figure \ref{figure:allowed_plus_minus , respectively.}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{tabular}{c} \includegraphics[width = 12 cm]{EtplotQplus.pdf} \includegraphics[width = 12 cm]{PhiplotQplus.pdf} \end{tabular} \caption{Upper panel: numerically produced NH time series, in $^\circ$, for the angle $\eta$. Lower panel: numerically produced NH time series, in $^\circ$, for the angle $\phi$. The values $a^\ast = +0.98, r_0=14.1\,R_g$, contained in the pale yellow region of the upper panel of Figure \ref{figure:allowed_plus_minus}, and $M=6.5\times 10^9\,M_\odot$ are used for the simultaneous numerical integration of \rfrs{dotI}{dotO} along with $\phi_0 = 17.85^\circ,\eta_0 = 291.7^\circ$, retrieved from Figure 2 $\boldsymbol{\ton{\mathrm{b}}}$ and Extended Data Figure 4 of \cite{2023Natur.621..711C}, for the initial conditions of $\phi$ and $\eta$. The time spans and the ranges of values on the vertical axes of both panels have the same length of those in Figure 2 and Extended Data Figure 4 of \cite{2023Natur.621..711C} for a better comparison with the latter ones. }\label{figure:etaphi_plus}
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{tabular}{c} \includegraphics[width = 12 cm]{EtplotQminus.pdf} \includegraphics[width = 12 cm]{PhiplotQminus.pdf} \end{tabular} \caption{Upper panel: numerically produced NH time series, in $^\circ$, for the angle $\eta$. Lower panel: numerically produced NH time series, in $^\circ$, for the angle $\phi$. The values $a^\ast = -0.95, r_0=16\,R_g$, contained in the pale yellow region of the lower panel of Figure \ref{figure:allowed_plus_minus}, and $M=6.5\times 10^9\,M_\odot$ are used for the simultaneous numerical integration of \rfrs{dotI}{dotO} along with $\phi_0 = 17.85^\circ,\eta_0 = 291.7^\circ$, retrieved from Figure 2 $\boldsymbol{\ton{\mathrm{b}}}$ and Extended Data Figure 4 of \cite{2023Natur.621..711C}, for the initial conditions of $\phi$ and $\eta$. The time spans and the ranges of values on the vertical axes of both panels have the same length of those in Figure 2 and Extended Data Figure 4 of \cite{2023Natur.621..711C} for a better comparison with the latter ones.}\label{figure:etaphi_minus}
elements.sent-2411-045: It can be noted that the NH prograde signatures displayed in Figure \ref{figure:etaphi_plus agree well with their measured counterparts of Figure 2 $\boldsymbol{\ton{\mathrm{b }$ and Extended Data Figure 4 of \cite{2023Natur.621..711C , both qualitatively and quantitatively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: It is not the case for the NH retrograde signals of Figure \ref{figure:etaphi_minus which, if on the one hand, retain the same sizes of those in Figure 2 $\boldsymbol{\ton{\mathrm{b }$ and Extended Data Figure 4 of \cite{2023Natur.621..711C , on the other hand, appear to be out of phase with respect to the latter ones.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: As far as the spin--orbit tilt angle $\psi_\mathrm{jet}$ is concerned, both its theoretically predicted value and its temporal evolution, calculated by integrating \rfrs{dotI {dotO as before, do not change with respect to their LT--only counterparts, shown in Figure 5 of \cite{2024arXiv241108686I , which were already found to be in agreement with their measured values.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Indeed, it has been shown that not only the hole's spin dipole moment $J$ through the Lense--Thirring effect but also its mass quadrupole moment $Q_2$, both calculated according to them, are able to explain the recently observed phenomenology of the jet precession of the supermassive black hole lurking at the centre of the galaxy M87.
elements.sent-2411-049: The inclusion of the precession induced by $Q_2$, calculated perturbatively as the Lense--Thirring one, yields to a modification of the allowed regions in the parameter space spanned by the hole's spin parameter $a^\ast$ and the effective radius $r_0$ of the accretion disk, assumed tightly coupled with the jet, with respect to the Lense--Thirring only case.
elements.sent-2411-050: Indeed, while neglecting $Q_2$ one obtains two identical permitted branches in the $\grf{a^\ast,r_0}$ plane which are symmetric with respect to the $a^\ast=0$ axis, the introduction of the hole's oblateness breaks such a symmetry since the allowed branch corresponding to retrograde hole's rotation gives larger allowed values for $r_0$ with respect to the prograde one.
elements.sent-2411-051: Furthermore, the presence of $Q_2$ tends to shrink (enlarge) the disk for prograde (retrograde) rotation with respect to the scenario in which it is omitted.
elements.sent-2411-052: The simultaneous numerical integration of the averaged equations for the spin dipole and mass quadrupole rates of change of the angles determining the orientation of the disk's orbital angular momentum with $a^\ast=0.98$ and $r_0=14.1$ gravitational radii allows to reproduce all the qualitative and quantitative features of the corresponding measured time series.
elements.sent-2411-053: It is not so for negative values of the hole's spin and the corresponding disk's radii which return out of phase theoretical signatures with respect to the empirical ones, while maintaining the same amplitudes.
elements.sent-2411-054: Finally, the value of the spin--orbit tilt angle between the angular momenta of the hole and the disk remains unchanged, along with its constancy over time.
elements.sent-2411-055: No new data were generated or analysed in support of this research.
elements.sent-2411-056: I declare no conflicts of interest.
elements.sent-2411-059: It has recently been proven \citep{2024arXiv241108686I that a simple perturbative calculation to the first post--Newtonian (1pN) order of the Lense--Thirring (LT) effect on the orbit of a test particle moving about a massive spinning object is able to reproduce, both qualitatively and quantitatively, several features of the jet precession in the supermassive black hole (SMBH) M87$^\ast$ recently measured with the Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) technique \citep{2023Natur.621..711C .}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Aim of this paper is to show that, actually, also the mass quadrupole moment\footnote{The possible impact of electric charge was recently investigated \citep{2024arXiv241107481M in the framework of the Kerr--Newman metric \citep{1965JMP.....6..915N,1965JMP.....6..918N as well.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The imprints of the precession of the latter on the SMBH-accretion disk system was recently investigated in \citep{2024arXiv241010965C .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The introduction of the hole's quadrupole does not spoil the agreement between the perturbative analytical calculation and the characteristics measured in \citep{2023Natur.621..711C , yielding to different allowed regions for the hole's spin parameter and the radius of the effective circular orbit modeling the precessing disk.}
elements.sent-2411-063: As a result, the celebrated \virg{no--hair (NH) theorems \citep{1967PhRv..164.1776I,1971PhRvL..26..331C,1975PhRvL..34..905R receive a strong support.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: According to the latter ones, the mass and the spin moments $\mathcal{M ^\ell$ and $\mathcal{J ^\ell$ of degree $\ell$ of a rotating BH \citep{1970Natur.226...64B , whose external spacetime is described by the Kerr metric \citep{1963PhRvL..11..237K,2015CQGra..32l4006T , are connected by the relation \eqi \mathcal{M ^\ell + i\,\mathcal{J ^\ell= M \ton{i\,\rp{J {cM ^\ell,\lb{nohair \eqf where $i:=\sqrt{-1}$ is the imaginary unit, $c$ is the speed of light, and $M$ and $J$ are the hole's mass and spin angular momentum, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: For a Kerr BH, it is \citep{1986bhwd.book.....S \eqi J =\chi \rp{M^2 G {c ,\,\left|\chi\right|\leq 1,\lb{Jei \eqf where $G$ is the Newtonian constant of gravitation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Furthermore, the mass quadrupole moment $\mathcal{M ^2$ of degree $\ell=2$, renamed $Q_2$, is \eqi Q_2 = -\rp{J^2 {c^2 M .\lb{Q2 \eqf If $\left|\chi\right|>1$, a naked singularity \citep{1973CMaPh..34..135Y,1991PhRvL..66..994S without a horizon would occur, implying the possibility of causality violations because of closed timelike curves.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Although not yet proven, the cosmic censorship conjecture \citep{1999JApA...20..233P,2002GReGr..34.1141P states that naked singularities may not be formed via the gravitational collapse of a material body.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The unit vectors $\boldsymbol{\hat{l }$ and $\boldsymbol{\hat{m }$, which lie in the orbital plane and are mutually orthogonal, are defined as \citep{Sof89,1991ercm.book.....B,SoffelHan19,2024gpno.book.....I \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{\hat{l}} \lb{elle} :=\grf{\cos\Omega,\sin\Omega,0},\acap \boldsymbol{\hat{m}} \lb{emme} := \grf{-\cos I \sin\Omega, \cos I \cos\Omega, \sin I}.\end{equation}.}}}
